params.text:
Principles of safe handling and good posture

Even when equipment does most of the work, good body mechanics protect you. The core principles include:

  • Stable base: stand with your feet apart, one slightly forward, to give yourself balance.
  • Bend your knees, not your back: lower yourself by bending at the hips and knees and keep the natural curve of your spine.
  • Keep the load close: hold what you are moving near to your body — the further away it is, the more strain on your back.
  • Avoid twisting: turn by moving your feet, not by twisting your spine while holding a load.
  • Move smoothly: no sudden jerking or snatching; move at a controlled pace.

Remember: reading these principles is not the same as being able to do them safely. Practical, trainer-led sessions are where you learn and are assessed on real technique.

params.taskDescription: Complete these key principles of safe posture and body mechanics.
params.textField: Keep a stable *base* with your feet apart. Bend at the *knees* rather than the back. Hold the load *close* to your body, and never *twist* your spine while carrying it.

params.question: You need to turn to place something to your side while holding it. What is the safest thing to do?

answers.text: Move your feet to turn your whole body

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Turning with your feet keeps your spine aligned and avoids the twisting that commonly causes back injury.

answers.text: Keep your feet still and twist at the waist

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — twisting the spine while loaded is a leading cause of back injury. Move your feet instead.

answers.text: Bend forward from the back to reach across

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — bending from the back puts high strain on the spine. Keep the load close and turn with your feet.

answers.text: Lift the load higher first, then swing round

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — swinging a load is uncontrolled and risky. Turn by stepping round with your feet.
